utterances: The Rockefeller Foundation is one organization that's helping those efforts.
They started a fellowship program in New Orleans for urban planners.
Talented men and women from around the country will spend two years constructing a blueprint for rebuilding New Orleans.
Carey Shea is a Rockefeller Foundation associate director.
She heads the foundation's New Orleans Initiative.
And Shawn Escofferey is an urban planner and was recently awarded a Rockefeller fellowship.
He also has a new job as deputy director with the New Orleans Neighborhood Development Collaborative.
Welcome to you both.
Thank you.
Hello.
So, Carey, let me start with you.
Briefly, tell me about the foundation's New Orleans fellowship program.
What kinds of people did you reach out to for example?
Well, the fellowship program is really based on one that was started at the University of Pennsylvania several years ago.

We reached out to people throughout the country who had community development experience, who were willing to make a serious commitment to New Orleans and who really wanted to learn how to or contribute skills they already knew or had to the rebuilding effort.
So you live and work in New Orleans, Carey, why does so much of the city still look like it did a year ago?
There are number of reasons for this.
The cleanup of the city took quite a long time.
There were mountains and mountains of debris that had to be removed from the city.
In addition, we have to remember that the - as much as things were destroyed at the surface level, things were also destroyed, infrastructure was destroyed below the surface.
So our water lines and sewers and gas lines were also all destroyed.
In addition, there's been a lot of bureaucracy that has been cumbersome and hard to navigate.
And the city is only now, beginning to see some of the rebuilding funds in town.
Shawn, you're a recipient of one of these fellowships.
What's your game plan?
I know you haven't really dug all the way in yet.
But how are you even going to start researching what you think should be researched?
Well, you know, I've done a lot of background research already.
I've been familiar with New Orleans for the last couple of years prior to Katrina, working with affordable housing development projects down there.
So I sort of had a sense of the lay of the land.

In terms of helping with the redevelopment process, I really wanted to make sure that the residents are truly involved intimately in all of the redevelopment and making sure that they still have a stake in the city.
And what do you think is going to do that?
We've heard a lot of people talk about schools, talked about safety, the air, the buildings.
There's so many things to approach.
Are you looking at this holistically or are you going to focus on one aspect?
I just want to let people who've tuned in midway know we're continuing our series on addiction.
We're talking about family intervention.
Of course, this is NPR's News & Notes.
I'm Farai Chideya.
We were just hearing from Charlotte, who had an intervention with her brother, and the person she got to do that intervention was Dr. Henry Gregory, director of the Rafiki Consortium.
We're also talking to Michael Middleton, a recovering heroin addict.
And Michael, let me talk to you.
Yes.
Who were you before your addiction, and how did addiction creep up on you?
Well, I practiced law for 25 years, and I was happily married, had two children, a boy and a girl, and pretty active in my community.
As a result of, I guess, domestic problems, I took to drugs, initially recreational and to fill up space or the void.
But then afterwards the drugs took over and I went on a 15-year run of going from cocaine, freebasing, to crack, and then finally to heroin.
Obviously I lost - my right to practice was suspended, so I couldn't practice law any more, and the whole grip of addiction took over.
And I was out there for 15 years.
When you say out there do you mean that you were jobless, and what was your day-to-day life like once you really were no longer able to practice law, and still in the throes of your addiction?
Well, the drug became the most important aspect of my life.
And I did any and everything to get drugs.
And obviously, being caught up in addiction, I could not do the nature of work which I think intellectually I was capable of doing.
And so I sought employment from any and everything.
I washed dishes.
I cut grass at times.
When it snowed, I shoveled snow.
I worked as a hustler with home improvement groups or companies.
And there were long bouts of unemployment.
The drug became the primary thing for me, it ran my life.
And I did any and everything I could to get heroin in the end.
When you mentioned your daughter you had this tone in your voice about her, a very loving tone.
How did she interact with you around your addiction, and now around your recovery?
Well, I have two sons, but one daughter.
And she's a daddy's girl.
She was about 29 years old when she caused me to seek treatment.
My daughter eventually became a lawyer herself.
I don't care what I was doing during the course of my addiction.
My daughter would find me.
She went off to school for college, and then eventually to law school, but she would find me.
She would seek her daddy.
One evening she surprised me and came to my apartment, the place that I was staying, and I had just used.
And we tried to have an intellectual conversation, and I went into a deep nod as a result of my using heroin.
I came out of the nod when I heard her crying daddy, and started crying.
The one time before I heard my daughter use daddy in that tone was when she was six years old and had fallen out of a car.
And I was not there at that time, and I came to the hospital.
As I was walking past her place in the hospital she cried out my name in that same way.
From the time period I heard that, I cried half the night.
I couldn't wait to seek help that morning, and from that day since I have not used drugs, or wanted to touch drugs.
Dr.

Gregory, when you hear this story, when you hear Michael's story about how family and his relationship with his daughter brought him back from the brink, do you think that that's a story that is the most of the time story as opposed to the rare story?
How does family really play out most of the time?
Well, I think it's very characteristic of how family plays out.
Matter of fact, go to the first part of the story when he talked about his addiction being to him, to some extent, a response to his domestic issues.
Addictions are just a symptom of other issues that we're going through.
Addictions are the way that we try to avoid pain and discomfort.
Much of the pain and discomfort in our lives is a result of our interaction in our families and in our social groups.
If you think about it personally, if you think about where your pain is right now, it probably has something to do with your relationships.

So, it's not feeling adequate, feeling unsuccessful, feeling some pain in those relationships sometimes that spark people going into drugs as a way to mask those feelings that they think are uncontrollable or overwhelming.

And on the other end, the family is also part of the solution because when you can mend those relationships, when you can help people communicate and get proper boundaries, when you can help them to share resources and share a common vision, then the healing can begin to occur, and the family can provide the support that it really wants to, and is trying to do all along.
Charlotte...
Now, this is so true - I'm sorry.
Please, go ahead.
I was going to say that this is so true.

I thought one of the things that happened in my treatment process was addressing the issue of my feeling less than anything because of my irresponsibility as a father to both my daughter and to my son.

Fortunately, one of the aspects of the treatment that I was given was dealing with my mental health, and addressing how I felt so inadequate, and trying to have me to realize the good person that was there.
But also my family, which has always been a loving family, I have 11 siblings who have always loved me, always loved me, and through the time period of this recovery expressed that even more.
It's never been the family that was negative, it was me.
And their love at times came through.
And since recovery, I think they have been a healthy aspect on this road for me.
Charlotte, you're a very successful professional, and we won't go into all of your history, but you're someone who's had a lot of success.
And when you think about the price that you and your family had to pay to really try to deal with these issues, was it worth it to you?
But the moon is way beyond that.
(LAUGHTER)
That's right.
So this is, like, kind of a dangerous part of space, right?

When those interactions become unbalanced from the oil, then you're prone to seeing, you know, new diseases emerge, predator-prey relationships change, temperatures change, and chemistry of the ocean change.
And all those indirectly affect the health of organisms.
Experts say since dolphins are at the top of the food chain, they reflect what has happened to their environment.
Definitely worth it, definitely worth it.
The air between myself and my brother, and the air between that brother and our other brother is much clearer.
I learned a lot from the intervention.
I learned a lot from Dr. Gregory.

He talked so much about being able to own your feelings, do what you need to do even if it seems hurtful, even if it seems standoffish, how to protect yourself, and yet still love and care for the person who's going through the addiction.
So, I do think it's worth it.
I don't know whether a full, complete communication of trust will ever be there, but at least a communication that seems loving is somewhat restored, so that's a good thing.
And my brother seems to be very, very happy that he's not where he was before.
So, yes, it was successful.
Like I said, my mother has passed on.

I know when she - before she died she was very glad that yes, he had at least broken that road that he was on, he'd broken that track record, but I wish she would be around now to sort of see where he is now.
Charlotte, I have to end it there.
But I really think all three of you have done so much to help us think through these issues.
Thank you.
You're welcome.
Yes, you're welcome.
My pleasure.
We were speaking with Charlotte, who participated in an intervention for her brother led by Dr. Henry Gregory, who's director of the Rafiki Consortium.
Also Michael Middleton, a recovering heroin addict.
All three joined us from the studios of WYPR in Baltimore, Maryland.
And coming up we have sports and your letters.
From the studios of NPR West, this is Day to Day.
I'm Alex Chadwick.
Coming up, she's the talk of Beijing now, the long distance swimmer, an athlete who is also an amputee.
First, there's news from Iraq, and in a sense, it's news because it shouldn't be, the kind of thing that happens in normal countries all time.
The prime minister from neighboring Lebanon is in Baghdad for a visit.
He's just the second Arab leader to make a visit to Iraq since the invasion five years ago.
At a press conference, Prime Minister Fuad Siniora said that it's time for the Arab world to work together again.

(Through translator) We are expecting great challenges in the near to middle future across the Arab lands, but we cannot face those challenges unless there is more cooperation between our two countries.
NPR's Lourdes Garcia-Navarro is on the line from Baghdad.
Lourdes, welcome back to Day to Day.
These are nice words from the prime minister, but what does it really mean?
Well, Iraqi Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki said that the visit is a sign of improving relations between the Arab world and Iraq.
He said the visit represents a return of normality to Iraq.
But these visits are for now clearly symbolic, but the symbolism is really important.
Since the U.S.-lead invasion, Iraq's Arab neighbors have politically isolated this country.
No Arab leaders came to visit.
No embassies were opened.
Iraq was essentially treated as a kind of pariah.
They were deeply suspicious of Iraq's Shiite-led government.
They thought that it was sectarian in nature at best, and at worst it was a puppet regime, backed by the Americans and in the thrall of Iran.
And while these Sunni-Arab led states, like Saudi and Jordan and Syria and the Golf states were refusing to really work with the Shiite government here, Iran did step in.
They've had an embassy here for a long time, an ambassador here for a long time.
Iran's President Mahmoud Ahmadinejad came here in March with a lot of fanfare.
Only slowly have these Arab states come around.
How much of a role is the U.S.
playing in prompting these visits?
There was a earlier visit from King Hussein in Jordan.
There was.
He was the first Arab leader to come and visit, and that only took place 10 days ago.
So I think it's a real sign how deeply suspicious Iraq's Sunni Arab neighbors were.
Also, a sign that things are changing.

And the Americans have tried desperately for these many years to get many of these countries to get involved in brokering negotiations between the Sunni and the Shiites, and they really haven't stepped up to that.
But we're seeing it happening more and more.
And I have to say the United States is very, very pleased.
